We never fully appreciated the need of such a work as we have
endeavoured to supply until its compilation was commenced.

While every insignificant event of alien and anti-Irish import
can find chronicles, while every imported pastime, and new-fangled
idea, can secure extravagant journalistic notoriety, the native

ames, and the National Association, founded to promote and
%qster them, are neglected, relegated to obscure corners, and
mistepresented in every detail and on every occasion.

Consequently, to collect accurate lists of consecutive Champion-
shipsfrom the “Irish Press” was an impossibility; and, unfortunately,

. owing to remissness, and changes in Gaelic officialdom, no
systematic records of winners were available.

We will, therefore, ask Gaels to deal leniently with any defects,
and aid us to rectify any mistakes which may mar the present
ANNUAL, and so assist in giving the G.A.A. a worthy chronicle,
and preserve its oldtime champion combinations from undeserved
oblivion.

We trust this first issue will meet, with approbation, and promise
to consider carefully any suggestions calculated to enhance its
utility, and increase its attractiveness.

The lists of County Champions (now for the first time brought

~ together in a permanent form) cannot fail to prove of interest,
- not alone at home, but in distant lands, where many, too many,
who once wore the Gaelic jersey of some Irish County team, will
have a host of memories recalled by the simple, talismanic name of
 their homeland combination.

~ We desire to thank all who aided in the compilation of this
~AxxUAL. Without their assistance it could not have been attempted.
- Many, at the expense of much local research, supplied us with
~ invaluable records; others generously placed at our disposal the
~ benefit of many years intimate connection with and experience of
- Gaelic games. ~ To them primarily the publication of the ANNUAL

33 We must especially acknowledge the courtesy of the Oireachtas
‘Committee of the Gaelic League in sanctioning the publication of the
- prize winning collection of Irish terms used in Gaelic games, and
?‘g e Rev. James B. Dollard (Sliabh na m-Ban) for his rousing poem
:éaﬁd earnest article.

The ANNUAL is designed to aid the progress of all that could
ustain national vigour, and promote racial pride. How far it
cceeds the Gaels at large will determine. For them, in exile and
home, it is intended ; to them it is humbly but lovingly dedicated.

THE GAELS.
(Specially written for the Annual.)
By Rev. James B. DoLLarD.

Here’s to the Gaels of Ireland
Their Country’s hope and pride,

In city or in hamlet,
By Suir or Shannon’s side.

In Desmond wide, or Ormonde
Tyr-Owen or Ossorie,

God save the Gaels of Ireland
Where'er their dwelling be !

The matchless Gaels of Ireland
So prompt at Banba’s call !
No knights more true to honour

E’er trod in camp or hall !
Such gentle hearts and stainless

Might quest the Holy Grail ;
God guard the Gaels of Ireland !

Their torch of Faith ne’er fail {

See, o'er the springing greensward
The swift opponents dash !
And hear that echoing music
The quiv’ring hurleys’ clash !
Insistent and resistless
They. press the stubborn goal—
Hark t—for the Gaels of Ireland,
Those thunder-cheers that roll !

Ye noble Gaels of Treland

Fair Banba needs you all !
Stand by your suffering Sireland
And wait the Battle-Call t
Then may your peaceful weapons
Be changed to shimmering steel,
And from your bristling vanguard
- Dismayed Oppression reel !

THE GAEL AND HIS INFLUENCE IN
AMERICAN ATHLETICS.

y AMERICAN ATHLETICS.

Tﬂn success of the American Athlete is the result of the influence of
 Gaelic sports in the United States. Prior to the Gaelic invasion, as
he American tour of the Gaelic champions, and crack hurlers, was
called, Columbia had none but sprint records, now she practically
holds them all, having won her laurels through the example, and by
“the aid, of Irishmen. As amateur athletic records extend a little
over half a century, it would be foolish to think that the Gaelic Invasion
‘was the first impression of the Irish athlete on American civilization.
‘The boys that fought for American freedom in the grim game of the
xevolnhomry war, learned the swift, sure stroke of victory on the
~ hurling fields of Treland.

ALL ROUND ABILITY.

~ The Irish name has always been associated with feats of strength,
endurance and agility, disregard for danger, aggressiveness and pluck,
_as oarsmen, boxers, weight throwers and jumpers. The claim that
 baseball is Trish in origin deserves credence, from the fact that men of
 Irish blood have been, and are, prominent in all the championship-
 teams.

HANDBALL.

- The Irish game of handball is very popular in America. There:
are handball courts in all the big cities, and most of the colleges. The
Olympic Club of San Francisco makes the game a speciality ; the
Young Men’s Christian Association reserve space in most of its
gymnasiums for the sport, and the professional high-salaried baseball
‘and boxing stars regard this Irish pastime as an excellent exercise to
keep fit.  Phil Casey, a native of Mountrath, Queen’s County, was
the first to make handball popular. He beat Barney McQuade for the
American Championship in 1868. He retained this honour until 1897,

The personality of both men,.and the high class exhibitions which.

“ GAELIC INVASION.”

: 'he  Gaelic Invasion ” was a sad affair for Ireland, for it robbed her
of some of her reddest biood and her best bone. The New York press
glowed with praise of the physique and bearing of the men, Their
- abstinence from tobacco and alcoholic drink was a subject for approving.
~ comment. The Trish athletes were the flower of Ireland’s manhood

- Maurice Davin of Carrick-on-Suir accompanied the team. The
late P. P. Sutton, who was afterwards Official Handicapper of the
.A.A., captained one of the hurling combinations, and represented
Sport of Dublin, as special correspondent. The country was in the
feﬁer of a Presidential Election, and the American athletic world was
et in conflicting sections. Despite these disadvantages, the Invaders
re met by thousands of their countrymen and friends.
itchell broke the hammer record, the athletes won many championships,
and the hurling exhibitions thrilled and delighted all. Pat Davin

Mumom champion miler, John McCarthy, all-round athlete, J. J.
Mo ooney, sprinter and long jumper, and W. Pendergast, Clonmel,
ne time all-Treland Secretary of the G.A.A.

HURLING AND FOOTBALL.

In the meantime the young men who left Ireland had been
accustomed to Gaelic athletics, and football clubs were organized.
There was a confederation of twelve or fifteen clubs formed by the
Trishmen in New York, Brooklyn, and Jersey City. Games were
played at Woodside, and Ridgewood, Long Island. The press was
diffident, but the events drew large crowds. The influence of the G.A.A.
was practically unknown outside the South and East, but it has extended
and will expand still further.

CONNEFF.

Tommy Conneff was a graduate from the Gaelic Association. He
was one of the best runners the world ever produced. He holds world’s
Tecords for the three-quarter, mile, and one and a-half miles. Conneff’s
distance was a mile, but he never seemed to know this until his career
was almost over. Jim Mitchell, who was training with the little Kildare
man, on the New York A C. grounds, told me a few days ago that
Conneff was equal to 4 minutes 11 seconds, on August 28th, 1895, the
day on which he ran the mile in 4 minutes 154 seconds, which still
Temains unbeaten.

ECROSS COUNTRY AND WEIGHTS.
[~ Mickey Kennedy, of Cashel, won the Cross Country Championship
of America in 1891. The finish was highly dramatic, for the Tipperary
man ran away from his rivals. He waved a flag over his head, and
shouted *“ Hurrah for Cashel” as he came to the post in a series of
buck jumps.

John Flanagan’s fame as a weight thrower has travelled to lands
where such sports are unknown. He has not yet seen his best, and his
greatest desire is to make some records on the soil of his native land
before he retires. Flanagan holds the following world’s records with
the 56 Ibs. :—from the side, two hands, no follow, from stand without
follow, both hands from a seven foot circle, no follow, and with unlimited
run and follow. His world’s record with the hammer was made at
Celtic Park at a sports meeting organized by the United Irish Societies
of New York to aid the educational work of the Christian Brothers in
Ireland, on July 31st, 1904, distance 172 feet 11 inches.

SHERIDAN.

The feats of Martin Sheridan at Athens last year brought joy to
every man of Irish birth or in America. He is a cousin of the late
P. W. Nally, and was born in Mayo. Sheridan won more points than

9

the entire representatives of the British Empire. He is an athlete
who has not yet’seen his best, and it is expected that he will raise the
record for the All Round this year. Sheriden’s best performance was
made in this event in Boston in 1905, for he broke the record by 480
points,scoring 6,800, Tom Kielywon this event twice—in 1904 and 1906.

PIONEERS OF PROGRESS—CELTIC PARK.

William Pendergast was the first to see that Gaelic Athletes should
have a park of their own. He was scoffed at by many to whom he
broached the subject, but the few loyal souls who aided his efforts
were worth a legion. The Celtic Park grounds were opened in 1898.
The place is ideal for athletics; a dancing pavilion and concert hall
enhauce the social attractions. Many weight and jump records were
broken here. The Club that organized the Celtic Park grounds was
known as the Greater New York Irish Athletic Association. It gradually
gathered a body of all-round athletes, and won honours in all the big
meets on the Atlantic seaboard. John Flanagan joined in 1901. His
coming marked the era of success that later made the Club the greatest
athletic organisation in the world. ~Celtic Park became a rendezvous
for all lovers of manly sports, and a Mecca for the exiled children of the
Gael. In those years there was no athletic ground fit to hold a champion-
ship meeting on, and the Irish ground was the scene of national and
collegiate contests. ~The work of the Gaels was an inspiration and
athletics attained great popularity. Clubs were organized, schoolboys
became interested, the Municipal Governments throughout the country
prepared public playgrounds, parks, baths, gymnasiums and athletic
fields. They are equipped with all facilities to develop athletics, and
maintained at public expense.
Tue Irisn Countes Araietic UNox was formed in 1904, [t
has made Gaelic Football very popular. The New York Daily News
~ offered two sets of gold and two sets of silver medals for the State
_ football and hurling championships, in June 1905. Sixteen clubs
entered for the football competition, and the contests were very exciting.
Kilkenny won the final from Cavan, The championship was conducted
~ on the same basis as that existing in Ireland. There was no hurling,
. 88its entry was not representative. Ireland’s national pastime does not
much support among the exiled Gaels. 1 often think our hurlers
Ireland too well to ever leave her green shores.  “Soccer”
ll made a great bid for American support in the fall of 1905,
the Gaels intend to capture the interest and favour of the Public
5 and Colleges. The Irish Counties Athletic Union attractions

This property was purchased by issuing bonds bearing 6 per cent.

DIFFICULTIES.

The Trish athletes must remember that sports are a small eloment
in America. Sunday is the only day that offers recreation. Many of
the best teams are beaten because their men must leave before the
play commences, or, perhaps, they cannot attend at all,

The influence of Gaelic Athletics among the Irish people in America
has been of inestimable good, and they have achieved many proud
 records in the arena of muscle. Such success is gratifying, but should
not be seductive, for despite all the triumphs and fame attained, there | in saying that the game of hurling is in the front rank of fine arts.”—
is more genuine pleasure and happiness in the most insignificant Michael Cusack in The United Irishman.
provincial Gaelic sports than in the greatest of America’s monster
fixtures. The Gael at home is the back bone of the race, and the envied
of his brothers in exile. W. FreTcHER.

THE NATIONAL PASTIME.

HURLING.

“Ts Hurling a Fine Art ? I think it is. I know nothing except the
Irish Language that gives more pleasure to those who take part in it,
or to those who concentratingly observe it. The mighty spirit of
Tolstoi was not more refreshed by his first sight of the mountains
after his journey over the vast plains of Russia, than is the soul of &
rue-born hurler at the sight of a good hurling match. When I reflect
n the sublime simplicity of the game, the strength and swiftness of the
players, their apparently angelic impetuosity, their apparent recklessness

In this connection, however, we must bear in mind that stature
may exist without vigour, or great vitality, as the records of abnormally
developed men prove, and that even moderate size may co-exist with
little corresponding athletic ability, as so many modern populations
demonstrate. We can understand, therefore, how in perpetuation of
the early physical vigour of a race, three factors play each a determining
part. First, the basis of strength, the race-cradle powers of the people ;
second, their physical and mental disposition, recreative customs, and
general habits; and third, their moral, social, and domestic regimes.

As regards the first, our earliest native authorities record, and
contemporary foreign writers confirm, the great physique of the race,
which remained dominant in Eire. The early stages of Irish colonisation
was a veritable struggle for “ the survival of the fittest,” and “the
fittest > remained not alone masters of Ireland, but practical suzerains
of Britain, not alone unmenaced by the all-conquering Roman legions
at home, but their daring and triumphant antagonists abroad. All
historical and traditionary data upon which we base our estimate of
the state of early Ireland, put beyond dispute two points—the
unsurpassed physical power and beauty of the inhabitants, and the
almost supreme sway of physical ability, whether attested in battle
fields by clans, or in athletic feats by individual champions. We can,
therefore, justly claim much for the fountain source of our racial
virility and vitality. It was primarily abundant, and in its exuberant
childhood laid well the basis of a lasting strain.

In examining the other factors in the physical growth and continuity
of our race, we have later authorities, and living evidence. Within the
region of accredited history we have abundant records of the wide-
spread athletic disposition of the Gael. Tn fact, in pre-Christian days
at least, physical exercises seemed the paramount manifestations of
popular feeling. There is no question but that the greatest events
in the pagan years of Ireland were the recurrence of the great funcral-
games, and aonaca, where athletic contests of the most comprehensive
character attracted King, Druid, Bard, Brehon, Chief, and Clansman,
brought together to legislate, barter, marry, make chronicles, give
judgment, exchange social amenities, and above all, acclaim the
champions in the contests of the feithche. Such events, like the Greek
Olympiads, were the outstanding events in the cycles of early Erin.
When the Christian creed came, little change took place. The old
admiration of athletic ability, the old human respect for the “strong
hand ” survived.

The character which these sports assumed is in itself a testimony
to the versatile nature of the Celt in the athletic arena—for scarcely

13

any present-day athletic event, which tests human strength, speed,
stamina or skill, and many long since obsolete, but were popularly
practised. Of course the use of arms, as then understood, and the
popularity and necessity of certain military services, accounted for
all those events peculiarly associated with contemporary soldiership,and
how formidable that soldiership was, let its antagonists tell. But on
the non-combative side there was a splendidly varied programme of
beneficial exercises, of which most survived until recent days, and the
effect of which are distinctly traceable in the physique of our country-
men in districts where intercourse with outside races has had a
minimum of influence.

Tt is above all a proof of the physical qualities of our race, and of
the fruitful traditions which guided it, that it was rather proficiency in
all branches, than excellence in any one, that secured princely reward.
Tn earlier days the warrior and champion proved his valour and
expertness in al/ branches, conditions, and feats ; and in this connection
Tady Gregory’s recital of Setanta’s taking arms is illuminating and
interesting reading for those to whom Irish records are not accessible
or practicable. The civilian athlete had the weights, and leaps, and
running in which to display his mettle, and the hurling and wrestling
and water feats as well. Those who had strength without speed, or
espertness of eye or hand without general suppleness of limb, secured
but little attention. The all-round ideal prevailed, and as the candidate
for the Fianna had, (as we read in Keating), to run with loosened hair
‘thro’ a wood, stoop under a branch lower than his knee, and leap higher
than his head, tread so lightly as not to break a decayed twig, and pull
a thorn from his foot without stooping, we may logically conclude
no dificulty was found in securing recruits of such expertness amongst
the Gaels of that day.

No one carefully studying early life in Ireland can iguore the
rominence which was given to the National pastime of Hurling in all
emblies. We have a few historic instances of the iniquitous
effects of badly borne defeats (a trait even noticeable to-day), and can
glean from the importance attached to the result, the high esteem
} in which the game was held. It was, in a word, the nearest approach
warfare consistent with peace. The anti-Irish will hail this as an
ission of the * brutality of hurling.” Tt is no such thing. It merely
to convey that hurling—played in a perfectly fair manner—
e to the tumultuous spirit of the Gael an zest surpassed only in the
1 of battle. I hope that zest will never decline, nor Hurling lose its
F t0 stir it, nor hurlers become devoid of their capacity to feel its
rating thrill, In Hurling in those far off days, s in war, individual

14

prowess, daring, and skill, had a full field for gelf-display, and many a
minor Diarmuid won the heart of some obscurer Grainne, by his deeds
on some less notable field than that of regal Tara.

Judged then, by the vicissitudes it has survived, the buffets it has
withstood, the drains of vitality it has endured, Gaelic physique is
pre-eminent and admirable. No fountain, however deep, no resource,
however great, could yield so much with undiminished flow, unless
replenished at its source. What was and could only yield this
exhaustive supply of muscle, generation after generation 2 The
pastimes of the people, of which history proves, and merit has retained,
Hurling as the chief in esteem, and foremost in benefit.

11— Fruir or THE TREE.”

This division of the subject may be the briefest, since there are few
who cannot give examples for themselves. I think it has been
conclusively shown what a factor our native pastimes have been in
creating and maintaining the undiminished vigour of the Gael, and that
Hurling was the popular team game of the Irish race. ‘We can, therefore,
claim for Hurling its place as the primary medium through which
Gaelic muscle and manhood derived its enduring pre-eminence.
Tnnumerable instances of individual athletic prowess prove the general
claim. Tt is, however, no part of my intention to select any individuals
for invidious distinction, and the necessary demonstration can be
completed without doing so. Munster has long maintained prominence
for its hurlers, Wexford and Kilkenny in Leinster, and Galway in
Connaught, coming nearest to the Southern province in hurling men.
Across a strip of country (practically co-terminous with the  Golden
. Vale ) Hurling has long been maintained at a high pitch of perfection,
and hence, too, has come forth a race of athletes unrivalled in the
world, in those two department in which the Gael seemed ever to
excel—the jumps and weights—strength and agility. This territory
might be termed a nursery of superb athletes. It has not had a
monopoly, the once widespread popularity of the native pastime
precluded that undesirable result; but for proof of my contention
this district is sufficient evidence. ~Here hurling has remained
indigenous to the soil, and athletic excellence has been its constant
co-attribute. And in a similar way do the other hurling centres exhibit
the same qualities in their athletes. Cork and Kerry, Clare and Galway,
Wexford and Kilkenny and Waterford—wherever an camdn has
retained its sway, physical supremacy and athletic ability have remained
vigorous and triumphant. Thereis no one acquainted with Irish Athletics
of the past fifty years, and more particularly since the advent of the

15

" G.A.A., but can readily quote the names of those whose personal
prowess forms the basis of athletic distinction for their native sod.

In contrast, take the other end of Ireland—where the Gael was
~ supplanted, native manners, customs and traditions uprooted, and
~ what is its record ¢ So adequately contrary as to be in itself a negative
~ proof of our contention. When has the North-East corner or even any
~ of the partly alienised counties of Ulster produced athletes capable of
* maintaining its honours in opposition to Liath Mogha ? Yet we know
~ that when Cpaot Ruao flourished, an camdn yielded not to Teéarhaip,
~ Caipeal, Cpuacan, Capman, &luinn or Ceanncopa® in mind or
* muscle. When the Gael declined in the wreck of his racial institutions,
~ and up-break of his national systems of life, he lost not alone his

insular, but his manly character. Such is the lesson and the warning

of history. Whether the divorce be voluntary or not, it is fatal, and

all the interests of our nation and people demand a closer clinging to

our native inheritances, for our safety, and honour, and glory alike.
~ Of those which concern all three, but more particularly the first, few
will gainsay that Hurling is at once the foundation and keystone of
our corporal nationality.

IIL—InTrINSIC MERITS.

So far we have dealt with historical proofs and general examples

of the beneficial influence and national prestige of Hurling. Yet I

admit it is quite possible that, notwithstanding all such, the game

 might not presently possess such attributes or merits as would entitle

‘WitnIN the past few years the progress made by the G.A.A. from

the Nationalist standpoint seems incredible. Four years ago, the

of strength, not alone in point of number of clubs, but also in public

> estimation, as has been evidenced by the large attendance at matches.
That the G.A.A. should take a strong, well defined part in the National

life of Ireland is absolutely essential, for with our population fleeing in

tens of thousands each year, the services of a single individual cannot.

be spared in the struggle for National existence, much less the priceless

County Committee, and an agreement to appoint no man to referee a

match who was nbt a member of the Association, would do much to
remedy this grievance if it were somewhat on the following lines. The
Association to consist of, say, two members from each club, from whom
the usual officers could be selected. The first duty of the members
would be to take the playing rules one by one, consider them from
different points of view, as to how breaches are committed, and agree
on a common, interpretation as well as a common penalty. Tt ehof\ld,
in the first instance; consist of men who had experience in refereeing,
and who would be prepared to impart their knowledge to others 'who
could afterwards be elected members, It must, of course, be admitted
that proper refereeing is the outcome of considerable experience, h|{t
at the same time, the old hands could give many useful hints to their
less experienced brethren. There is no gain saying the fact that at the
present time different men have different views regarding some of thle
rules and the penalties which should be imposed for breaches: This
want of uniformity is very trying on players, and does not improve
their temper. Tf there is anything more exasperating than another, it is

29

for players to meet a referee on one day who has a view of the rules
different to that held by a man under whom they played on previous
occasions.  Time after time I have observed this difference of opinion,
and have heard players complain of it. Uniformity of action amongst
referees has many advantages. It would, for instance, put a stop to
that unseemly language sometimes heard in Gaelic fields when players
accuse referees of partiality and other things, because, though, in some
cases such remarks are immediately taken notice of by some referees,
, others are only too ready to condone them on the plea of excitement.
A The spirit of self-control should be developed in each individual, but
i unfortunately it is sadly lacking in some players, and to those who are
indifferent to so essential a quality it should be made plain, that any
unseemly outburst of temper, either physical or oratorical, would meet
from each referee the maximum penalty for the time being—removal
from field. And if the circumstances warranted it, 2 recommendation
for suspension to the Committee having charge of the match. In this
connection, I may here remark, that if a referee made a strong report
against any man, it should receive the undivided support of the
committee, otherwise the object in view could not possibly be realised.
Above all things, it would be absolutely necessary that referees should
be men of thoroughly independent minds, with whom threats or cajolery
would not weigh, and who—once a decision was given—would not
alter or modify it under any circumstances. Decisions should also be
prompt, and the habit of giving the reason why a decision was arrived

severely in the early stages of a match, for the recent displays of Kilkenny
and Cork in hurling, and Kerry and Kildare in football, have demon-
. strated beyond doubt, that both games can be played in a most scientific
manner, accompanied by plenty of dash, without that roughness so
characteristic of some teams. A difficulty may now arise on the
question of refereeing Inter-County matches, but, to my mind, it could
‘easily be got over. It is true the style in each of the provinces is some-
what different, but an interchange of opinions through whatever medium
would be found most convenient, should dispose of this difficulty.
Now, as to the question of expense. At present rail and hotel expenses
are paid to referees, consequently it would be but a matter of
postage and stationery to the secretary with, perhaps, a small sum for

innumerable, I am sure each County Committee would do the needful.

~ This is but a brief outline of what I conceive to be a growing necessity.
ny details are omitted for want of space on the one hand, and want
ime on the other, to develop them, but I cannot help thinking that

if the refereeing of matches were placed in the hands of men banded

Wit the death of Michael Cusack, not alone died the Founder, but the
living embodiment of the G.A.A. Seldom have an institution and the
man who conceived it been so absolutely the reflection of each other.
The G.A.A. created no new order of being in Ireland. It only harmonised
and disciplined and gave aim to the existing elements of an athletic
race, and in Michael Cusack that race found a wonderfully appropriate
agent for the achievement of its aims, and the expression of its ideals.
By birth and location of birth, by temperament and evolution of
temperament, by sympathies and the cultivation of these sympathies,
by physique and the growing ambitions which accompany physical
virility, Michael Cusack was in every quality of mind, heart and body
fitted to reduce to order and raise to self-dignity the manhood of his
Tace.
Leaders of men often fall far below men’s conception of their personal
and intellectual qualities. Michael Cusack was not—nor ever posed as—
& leader of men, but as a medium for the creation of men, the offspring
of his heart and brain—the G.A.A.—is unrivalled.

Without that intimate aquaintance with the traditions of the race
in its pristine strength and beauty of institutions which Clare parentage
‘and intercourse gave, he would never have so adequately restored
the pride of native games to the native manhood. ‘Without that
knowledge of the youth of his time which his occupation afforded

36

him neither could he have adapted the olden caman so well to the

exigencies and capabilities of his day. Without a dual faith and hope—

firm and to the'last unshaken—in the merits of the games and the
. mission of his race, he would not have maintained so buoyant a heart
* amidst troubles of a personal, political and social character.

No obtrusion of other men or flaunting of other names can
minimise his labours or success, and to his latest hour he was as
vigourous in, and as proud of, his association as if the homage and
wealth of the world were his. Indeed, for either he never catered or
cared. To many, enthusiasm such as his, which neglects personal
prosperity to pursue an idealis akin to folly ; out of such wise men
nations are not made, and upon the annals of such censorious and
prudent beings no heroism glows with purifying ray.

Michael Cusack as a Gael—a constructive Nationalist—did his
part. The G.A.A. remains a monument to his memory, which is, in
itself a treasure to those privileged to be reckoned with his personal
friends.  To none but those favoured with such intimacy were the
splendour of his mind and childlike laughter of his soul known. To
the enemies of his ideals he was unsparingly severe, but the bitterest
wrath and most crushing invective fell on false friends and com-
promising Gaels. Between the man of storms confronted with opponents
or renegades, and the man of joyous happiness when expanding in the
companionship of congenial friends, the contrast was great. To the
latter recollection is now too pathetic to brook the seeming adulation
of words, the sacredness of such friendship and the memory of such
intercourse are something too overpowering or evasive to be moulded
into English speech.

God rest you, a tilieal, giant heart, kindly soul,and heroic intellect,
The peace and joy that are depicted so fascinatingly in those Gaelic
legends you loved so well must now assuredly be yours. If the tempests
ploughed the ocean of life on your voyage through, the smile of the
dawn and the blessedness of the calm were thine.  In one heart, at
least, thy memory is radiant with nobility and gratitude, of acquired
devotion, and wisdom which you lavished so generously on all, and
(like more material things) hoarded so sparingly for self. The generosity
of the Gael, like his joyousness, is beyond the measure of meaner
mortals,

After, and in the path made broader by your passage, we will follow,
brave soul—moulding the mind, the muscle, and the manhood which
will fill the ranks of Gaeldom, despite of foes who would poison our
Tace’s vitality at its source, or friends who would barter its inheritance
at their evil behest.

DAN (’CALLAG:

PeruAPs the severest blow the Dublin Hurling
received was the untimely death of Dan O’Callaghan.

Dan was the League—the living and unfailing personification
vigor, progress and ideals. Tt is not because his most unselfish activiti
were directed towards its success, his loss is here deplored, but because
it secured the sincerest and most unsparing affection of his great Gaelic
heart and the greater share of his ceaseless and beneficial exertions.

His candour, his energy, his constructive ability and capacity for
surmounting obstacles were the foundation and support of the League.

How he accomplished all he did—working, playing, organising and
administering—can only be marvelled at, and will find few, if any
emulators.  Unfortunately the strain told, and the unselfish spirit
and generous heart succumbed to the self-imposed and onerous burden
of Gaelic zeal.

Tt would be an attractive and gratifying labour to dwell at length
upon Dan O'Callaghan’s many-sided activities and single-minded
ambition—a task, however much the affections of the writer might
tempt him to essay, his Jimited abilities and space compel him to refrain.
To all who knew him, Dan needs no tribute to ensure affectionate
recollection, and the sorrow of Dublin Gaels in particular, and of the
Gaels of all Treland in general is even yet too keen to admit of the ful)
effects of his loss being analysed and deplored. His was a heart to
imitate in endeavour, zeal and honesty, in detachment from sections
and allegiance to principle, in unreserved love of our Gaelic games,
and in unsparing labours for their progress and promotion.

* * * * * * *

We trust the committee having charge of the Memorial Cross,
which it is proposed to erect over his grave, will receive the prompt and
generous support of all true Gaels, for in one way or another—for
practical benefits or for lessons of Gaelic spirit, all Gaeldom is indebted
to Dan O’Callaghan.

CATCHING: A PLEA FOR ITS ABOLITION.

Havine played and studied closely Gaelic football for the past twelve
years, T desire to direct attention to one aspect (and, in my opinion,
a grave defect) of the game ; T mean the catching of the ball. While
fully conscious of the many points from which the game may be
viewed, I am convinced this defect is a grievous one, without any
redeeming feature, and one that calls for immediate remedy. Its
continuance will hamper the development of the game to a regrettable
extent. Its removal would revolutionise the style of play, and
redouble the popularity of our football code. It would, moreover,
constitute no wanton attack upon any peculiar characteristic of the
Gaelic game.

The old “ parish against parish, from ditch to ditch” methods,
irrespective of, or rather anterior to rules, are all we have had to go
upon in framing the present laws, so that in football we have no
historical qualities to preserve, nor traditions to guide us. It may
devolve, therefore, on the present gerieration of Gaelic footballers to
mould a game suited to the temperament, strength, speed and agility
of young Irishmen of to-day and to-morrow.

We have, however, a valuable—if recent—experience to draw upon.
During the regime of the G.A.A. the game has undergone many
changes ; for instance—size of ground, scoring area and ball, number
of players, manner of refereeing, duties of umpires, linesmen, etc. So
that there can be no sentimental reluctarice to adopt a still greater
reform which, while it will be immeasurable in its benefits, will not
be revolutionary in its tendency or effects.

One feature has remained unaltered throughout all the years and
unaffected by changing laws—the dash, vim, and courage, agility,
speed, and strength of the men who play the game. It is in the con-
viction that the reform T advocate will develope these qualities that
I invite attention to my views.

One of the greatest impediments to the full development of the
game is the effects of the present permissive catching of the ball.
You cannot, according to-rule, charge a man, except he is in the act
of playing the ball. Here is placed a premium on, and a barrier around
the player who comes into possession! So that possession and not
playing is the aim uppermost now with many Gaelic footballers.
Playing strictly in harmony with the rules, you encourage timidity,
“flash ” play, and rob the game of its essential quality (as a robust

41

game) of vigour, or played in conflict with the rules, you give an
I incentive to vicious charging and incessant melees.  Under such
conditions a player cannot be charged—no matter how fairly—
for if he falls—voluntarily or involuntarily—a penalty is incurred !

2 Again, players hold the ball deliberately to induce opponents to
hold them—result, a free to him in possession! Ts this good football
as Gaels understand it ?

Take the fair, conscientious player, anxious to avoid penalties
who comes in contact with an opponent in possession. What chance
has he of playing the ball without risking a free?  He must either
make a palpable pretext of charging or start childishly and futility
slapping the ball or its possessor; which looks silly, and is certainly
not football. Tt never succeeds in dispossessing an opponent who
twists away and kicks, or drops the ball ; or is cornered. A scrimmage
and a deadlock results, relieved only by the referee’s whistle. The
problem of a restart is then solved most likely by a free to the real
culprit, ergo—the player first in possession.  This justly irritates,
and if it leads directly to a score, has a most undesirable effect upon the
game.

Now take the case of the man in possession and in the act of playing,
the ball. Under cover of securing the ball, but really with the covert
object of charging him, his opponent comes along, and collides with him.
Here the very serious onus rests on the referee of destinguishing whether
that charge was a vindictive one for the man and not the ball,or merely
- the impetuosity and dash of the Gael. In the heat of a contest no Gael
can so desist. Then give him scope for his dash and vim, which is the
admiration of all true Gaelic men and the terror of weaklings. Under
‘the present rule you impose an unnatural restraint upon him which
he cannot endure and do himself justice as a player.

There is no middle course : either allow the player who catches and
- holds the ball to be charged, held and handled (an undedirable resource)
o else abandon this catching, which deadens the ball, kills the game,
makes the man, not the ball, the object of attack, and, therefore
engenders bad blood and retaliation.

As a remedy I would suggest the abolition of catching and the
substitution of handling. ~Here 1 might, perhaps, draw an analogy
between hurling and football. In the former game fouls are rare,
compared with those which occur in the latter. This, I submit, is due
the facts that the ball, and not the player, is the main interest,
that a player is seldom in actual possession of the ball.  The
gies of the players in hurling are concentrated on the play. It
none of them anything to charge an antagonist foully ; and the

42

most adroit attempt to do so is easily seen.  Except in the open a
hurler seldom catches a ball. If he does so in the close he is about the
most awkward man in the field, and none knows it better than himself.

The mere catching of a ball has never presented difficulties toan
expert footballer. Tn the past, a good catch was necessary, but in my
opinion, never stamped a man as a first-class player.

The allowance of one hop with both hands to clear after catching

and four paces while in possession failed to relieve the resulting dead-
lock. A hop to clear may be all right to dodge a single man, but
hopping in a cluster is bad—for the hopper never sees the ball agrin.
Abuse of the four pace limit is being carried too far, especially in the
forward line, where the probability of a score being brought off
exasperates the backs, and their efforts to cope with the privileged
player in possession result in a foul, a free, and further trouble. This
is a result of temperament and not of discipline, and too much is asked
of any Gael when he is desired to permit an opponent to run round him
without giving him a fair means of utilising his resource, strength,
speed or dash. Under the present rule he cannot prevent an opponent
Ficking, except by fouling.  We have been watching the abuses arising
from this particular source, and by far the greater number of penalties
awarded are due toit. Have these penalties and free kicks had effect ?
Certainly not, the practice shows no diminution, the penalties tend
regularly to increase. If a penalty does not act as a deterrent, half its
justification is lost. How long must our otherwise admirable game
be spoiled by such futile expedients” as are now adopted to repair a
remediable defect ?

My remedy then—“not to catch but to handle” has innumerable
advantages. Tt would preserve all the characteristics and merits
of the Gaelic game while eliminating its chief defect. Tt would conduce
to more and better ground kicking (a sadly neglected feature), give more
scope for dash, fake the game more open, much faster and continuous,
and bring arms, hands and eyes into co-operative activity. A quick,
Topping ball, deitly handled, would enable a player to move faster and
get in much more scientific play than at present, and a vigorous box
or a skilful pass by hand would surely be preferable to the fumbling,
serambling tactics now cultivated ?

The change 1 have advocated is held by some with whom T have
discussed it as too drastic, and tending towards still further “in-
novations.” It is no more drastic than any of its predecessors—it
does not propose to alter radically the Gaelic game, and would, I
believe, result in giving the Gael two of the finest field games played
on the crust of the earth. T. H. REDMOND.
